item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, this one freaks me out, good job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic stuff all around. Not really sure how I didn't see this one sooner, honestly.
SCP-3034-A has now begun at 198.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. If this was your first skip, man have you got talent! The creepiness, the subtlety of the last incident report, everything is just… perfect.

If I could upvote twice I would.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so，so，so cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, good catch — I added that bit in a later draft and didn't think to check back on the spread. Will fix it — thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great article, and I second all the praise it has received.

One nitpick though, the SCP state that the shortest gap between broadcasts was two weeks, but incident 201 (which happened during a broadcast) and 202 (first broadcast to start at 199) seem to have occurred four days apart. Unless I'm badly misreading the date format somehow. Is 202 not being counted because it was assumed to be a result of the unofficial test during 201?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the point? People can think what they want?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… Is there a polite way to criticize someone's headcanon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question: Are non-anomalous children EXPLICITLY not allowed on SCP sites and I missed it somewhere, or is it just because non-anomalous children would normally (and with good reason — even in the vanishingly rare case of a child that would be qualified for a position with the foundation, I'm pretty sure the Ethics Committee, if it existed in that canon, would pitch a fit and even in canons where it doesn't others would for a variety of fully-justified reasons) be presumed to be civilians without appropriate clearance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have but one tiny quibble: seeing as the usual automated broadcasts are pretty much universally called "numbers stations", as referenced in the author comment, how come they're variously referred to in the scip as "counting station(s)" and "number stations"?

I mean, "counting station" is certainly a more correct term for this one, but the more mundane ones not so much. Or is it a direct translation from Russian?

All the rest of it, absolutely on the button.

And I wonder if it's got any relation to the other cold war / MAD type scips that are already knocking around - the time-locked ICBM, for example…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a headcanon that what the distortion sound is is that of a Pattern Screamer. Like, the thing that GRU-P made a deal with used a Pattern Screamer to craft this horrific thing. When it starts to scream, all the kids will be taken to it to also be Pattern Screamers and scream along with it. Essentially, this, if the girl counts down to 0, will open up a void that'll leak Pattern Screamers who are children into reality.

Anyway, this is a pretty good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant, creepy as f. The best bit is when you realise the longer the countdown goes, the more children are suffering.

I don't know what nightmare this is, and I never want to find out.

I do want to know what happens when the countdown reaches zero. God willing though, they'll never break protocol. Think of the children.

+1

PS Just read the discussion above about the interview. I dunno if it's already been edited but I think it's fine. It gives just enough background to know that it's gonna be a bad idea (TM) to let the countdown end, and gives some opaque reasoning for the girl doing the countdown. I also think that gives it a good anchor as a reason for the children in the piece. I'd definitely keep the interview in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering that they're working with this one rather than trying to kill it I doubt it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know that 1178 was them trying to destroy an anomaly with a nuke and this is them weaponizing one, but could this and 1178 be related at all?

We're leaving together,
But still, it's farewell.
And maybe we'll come back...
To earth; who can tell?

I guess there is no one to blame;
We're leaving ground.
Will things ever be the same... again?

It's the final countdown ~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The static is a pretty good idea! I'll mull it over, a bit — I'm now increasingly loathe to change the article considering it's well the year-mark in age (and I feel increasingly strange about changing articles the older they are), but the static idea does at least let me wipe over some of the parts of the article that retrospectively make me grimace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the interview is an important part of the article, it gives some tantalizing hints of what might be happening behind the scenes here. If you're not entirely happy with it, I'd suggest having pieces of the audio be lost to fire damage, but definitely keep the bits mentioning deals and sacrifices, the reference to a "'fable'", and choice parts of Sergei's last line:

► How I'd do it
▼ Close File

AUDIO LOG

NOTE: The following audio was recovered from partially burned magnetic tapes discovered on-site. All dialogue is translated from Russian.

[BEGIN LOG.]

[Shuffling papers.]

VOICE 1: Let's get this over with.

VOICE 2: [static] -empted to steal state property, Sergei. How did you think this would end?

VOICE 1: She is no- [static] -as a name.

VOICE 2: What was your inten- [static] -mericans? Let them know about our little project?

VOICE 1: You know me. You know I have served with honor and distinction. You know I would never —

VOICE 2: Did they promi- [static] -ink you would raise her as your own?

VOICE 1: This is wrong. You know this is wrong. You are meddling in powers you can't possibly —

VOICE 2: And do you think the Amer- [static] -t this 'atom-bomb' can do?

VOICE 1: I know that one does not make deals with atom-bo- [static] -ot sacrifice little g- [static]

VOICE 2: [static] -crifice that will save millio- [static]

VOICE 1: [static] -ericans even believe this 'fable' exis- [static]

VOICE 2: They will. We will show them. A small taste. Just as th- [static] -oshima, we will —

[Scuffling, followed by shouting.]

VOICE 2: I trust you will control yourself, now?

VOICE 1: (panicked) You cannot do- [static] -ightmares. I know you have had the- [static] -eaming in the dark. That's what it wants, Vaslov. That's what it is. [static] -ake a deal- [static] -ntained it, and now you want to offer it —

VOICE 2: Only if they force our hand.

VOICE 1: You can't — for the love of God, you can't —

[END LOG.]

Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, just wanted to say, I really enjoy this scp and how simple but still entertaining it is, and how you leave a lot to the imagination. This is a perfect example of a boring concept with a great execution that leads to a great final product. I also like how you gave number stations a interesting spin instead of just weird messages what are encrypted and no one knows how to decrypt the message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh OK thanks for the clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original aithor The Great Hippo, deleted their account, and then rejoined the site at a later date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a bit confused. Did the original author of this scp delete his account and then someone else took over and rewrote it? Or did the new author just add on to this scp?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a pretty good point, and something I'll try to add to the article in a bit; just having a phonetic footnote would be super-useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes sense, but I think it would be prudent to also include a phonetic pronunciation for the phrase in the containment procedures (maybe as a footnote). In the event that something goes wrong and no available personnel know any Russian for whatever reason (maybe they're all dead and it's an MTF responding), you want whoever is available to be able to easily know how to say it.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Good idea. I've already received the go-ahead from Hippo in chat to make this edit: I'll forward this to them.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
One of the characters in this - Agent Browning - has been used again by Hippo in a few other skips. (Namely, SCP-3054 and SCP-3241, as well as an offhand mention in SCP-3667.) Of these, 3241 is the one most about Browning with the most focus on him as a character, and it'd make sense to make that connection.

I suggest editing this:

I assembled a team consisting of myself, five of my men, and Agent Browning (selected for his proficiency in several Russian dialects). Once appropriate preparations were made, we set out to investigate.

To this:

I assembled a team consisting of myself, five of my men, and Agent Browning (selected for his proficiency in several Russian dialects). Once appropriate preparations were made, we set out to investigate.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: approved by author in chat

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Firstly, I propose a crosslink to the tale Countdown, since it is a tale about this skip.

Secondly, I propose a crosslink to SCP-2664. GRU Division "P" doesn't have a hub at the moment, and this is a GRU-P skip, so in its absense SCP-2664 can give the audience more information about the GoI.

Editing this:

We were first made aware of the site by a defecting agent of GRU-P.1

To this:

We were first made aware of the site by a defecting agent of GRU-P.2

Editing this:

I'm starting to wonder if this isn't some sort of elaborate prank.

To this:

I'm starting to wonder if this isn't some sort of elaborate prank.

Footnotes
1. GRU Division "P" (Psychotronics); a Soviet organization tasked with the acquisition, study, and development of anomalies for the benefit of the USSR.
2. GRU Division "P" (Psychotronics); a Soviet organization tasked with the acquisition, study, and development of anomalies for the benefit of the USSR. For more information about this Group of Interest, personnel with appropriate clearance may consult the documentation for SCP-2664.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concurring with stormbreath here. The interview is pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, wow — I had totally forgotten about that cross-link. Thanks for reminding me!

I might just end up tweaking it a little to take out the parts I find cheesy, in that case — yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that the interview may not be the best, but some of the information conveyed in it is flavorful and helped to define the skip (the humanization of the girl by saying she has a name, the comparison to an atomic bomb, the fact that there is dissent between members of the GRU-P) and improve it.

Also, given that the interview is the only part that implies that this is a doomsday device of some kind, I would advise against changing it - I feel that the story helps define it. Making it mysterious has its own appeal, but I feel that the more defined option works better.

If anything, I would recommend altering or revising the interview, not removing it.

It's also bears noting that one of the two tales that references this article (Some Are Born to Endless Night) is explicitly mentioning the interview when it crosslinks to this:

We see dark. We see light. We create strange images. Of a deal with a devil in a frozen land.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I've wanted to take the interview out of this skip for a while; my only reservation has been whether or not anyone who upvoted wouldn't have done so had the interview been missing.

I'm making this post to ask if there's anyone out there for whom the interview is important. To me, it's a bit cheesy and on the nose; I think readers get more out of having the anomaly just be associated with GRU-P, with no explanation as to what it's for.

If you disagree, feel free to say so here. Otherwise, I'll take it out in a few days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do think it would be interesting though if a child stumbled across the site and triggered something, or perhaps there's some historical evidence where, several decades ago, a number of children vanished at this exact spot.

I think the reason I did want to bring up these few annoyances is because the piece was of such quality, I hated to see anything even slightly detract. It's the sort of article that I've love to see tales or exploration logs or any supplemental material at all really - although there's always the risk of telling the reader too much and spoiling the effect!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression that this site's only SCP is 3034 due to it being a proximity-based anomaly, but even so, the examples you've given about children occasionally being on site make the caution more justified, given the non-zero likelihood. The fact that the anomaly never seemed to be upset by children nearby was my main gripe. But like Hippo says, it functions as an excellent hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I feel there's a pefectly reasonable place for "things that shouldn't really need to be said but we're going to do it anyway because we're paranoid." It would be very bad if a child was on-site, considerably more so than another Site. Such a line being in the containment procedures serves to emphasize that point - you absolutely do not want any kids on Site. I've always seen the Foundation as hyper-paranoid. Redundancy is encouraged when the fate of the world is on the line.

I could also think of a few situations in which children might be on a Foundation site, like:

  • D-Class children, like those sourced from SCP-1680 or the babies used in Khevtuul 1
  • The children of personnel who are living on-site, like the case in Portraits of Your Father
  • The Foundation is testing an anomaly which only affects children, like Bobble the Clown
  • A child is the anomaly, like SCP-3511

The last one is the most likely to come up - imagine if the anomaly was captured, and the MTF needed to get to a Site quickly. If the closest site was Provisional-3034, they might go there, but you wouldn't want that if the anomaly is a kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! xD

re: C1 certifications — the Foundation wants you to know how to count in Russian, too (after all, agents were at one point required to wait a while before sending the kill signal), and I imagine they want you to know Russian just in case this thing ever starts doing anything other than counting (so it can be immediately understood, and agents can determine what to do quickly). Also, if you're a native Russian speaker, I imagine getting a C1 certification is pretty easy! There's no reason the agents stationed there wouldn't be native Russian speakers; the containment procedures are just there to clarify that whoever you assign there, they must have at least C1 certification.

re: the "No kids" thing, there's no excuse for that; that's purely the author's hand popping into the skip and shoving a hook into the containment procedures. >_>

ETA: Like, I could spin some BS about this implying a prior incident we're not privy to, and I seem to recall that was my justification for it at the time — but honestly, the real reason it's there is to make the reader go "wait, WTF? why?".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this SCP, but "All personnel are to have at least C1 certifications in Russian." bothers me for two reasons. First, if all that is required to cease a transmission is a single Russian phrase, then you only need to know that phrase, not the whole language. And even if this was not true, I question the need for people with certifications in Russian. The SCP Foundation is an international organisation - why not assign native speakers?

"Under no circumstances are children permitted on-site." Also broke my immersion a bit. Non-anomolous children are already not allowed into Foundation sites - why would this be any different? And either way, just because a child is the one counting down, doesn't mean that another child in physical proximity would have any impact on this anomaly.

But these are just two little annoyances. This SCP did an excellent job of creeping me out, and leaves just enough unanswered questions to make me keep wondering about its nature long after I've stopped reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was just so excellent. A really good example of how to get the old-school creepy feel right without any cheese.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Very deserving of the rating. This was an excellent start to the body of work from one of my favorite authors on-site.
Somewhat fitting, I think. This is a great scip, and you should be proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! The end was actually changed (fairly recently; a week ago, maybe?) from its previous iteration. I think that its new (and very likely final) form is much, much punchier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooooh, that last line was great.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful article! Out of curiosity, is this inspired by any real Russian folklore?

item.title: Re: Here it is!
item.content:encoded: Wow this is your first one? It's a masterpiece one of the best scps I've ever read. The writing is excellent and the story is awesome. My first scp is currently in deletion. Your really amazing at writing scps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the minute I saw the line in the containment procedures about not allowing children on-site, I was looking forward to reading the rest. Excellent hook, great buildup, solid work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely adore this article for doing number stations in a unique way, although I do feel that the last addendum is very generic and unneeded.

Also, had a thought on what the weapon is meant to do; What if the weapon is meant to target children specifically? Considering how much shit both sides of the Cold War could have probably seen, a weapon that biblical in nature would probably cause instant surrender if threatened with it. And if you did have to use it, deleting a nations next generation would be a instant victory.

Additionally, why would they bother bargaining with some kind of unknown supernatural entity for just some basic widespread destruction when they have it already with nukes and such? Seems like they'd be working with something a few miles above "a bomb but really big and ghost powered".

item.title: Re: Here it is!
item.content:encoded: There has to be an in-universe reason for having a photo that is partially in color be in th article. It's too bizarre for me to not notice otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god, how is this a first article?

Fucking fantastic. You captured the real-life creeping horror of anomalous broadcasts and did a perfect job never showing the monster, as it were. I do wish articles where the Foundation knows something bad will happen, but not what, were more prevalent or widely accepted. I mean, even after that activation event, I'm pretty sure that's not what this is meant to do; she didn't get to zero, she just stopped and freaked out.

This is just really stellar. +1

item.title: Bravo!
item.content:encoded:
This hits the mystery/tension sweet spot. Doesn't give too much away - doesn't hold back too much either. I can't find any fault in it at all. Expertly done, it gets +1

I hope to see more from you in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, and if I'm going to be honest, I much prefer a Foundation that actually tries to treat D-Class personnel humanely. It's more interesting narratively, and probably reflects reality a little bit more closely. A lot of my perception of the Foundation was informed by their treatment of D-class, and a lot of the stuff I read about D-class came from earlier articles, so — that's probably why I read it the way I did.

Also, thanks for that link — that's a pretty great SCP. xd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That being said, if the problem is that Foundation personnel can be trusted to remain professional without resorting to threats of termination, then maybe I shouldn't have had a Foundation researcher decide to perform an unsanctioned test in the first place?

I think that part is fine. Especially if there wasn't an explicit prohibition in the original document, which is how I imagined it.

There are many headcanons on this site, so there's no one true interpretation. I see the SCP section as a sort of cheat sheet for people who aren't familiar with the anomaly. It's the official rules, sure, but the researchers who know what's going on (or think they do) might not always follow it exactly.

The guy who did the unauthorized test decided not to follow orders because he knew there wasn't a real reason for them. Arrogant, sure, but not too out of character for a foundation researcher. The next person will have read the incident report and will know why that rule exists; they won't be making that mistake again.

Edit: regarding your other comment, keep in mind that the tone has shifted quite a bit since the early days. Series 1 articles often treat D-class like paper towels: just throw them at the problem until it's better. Newer articles tend to be more humane. Personally my headcanon is the latter - besides the moral issues, it just doesn't make sense to waste lives so carelessly.

Edit2: SCP-2130 is basically a meta critique of this issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D: Uber predator that focuses on children? if this was portrayed in almost any other way i believe it would not reach this sites standards. Well done. This article scares me more than any other on this site, and i can assure you that.

Though, i do have a question, what do you believe this… thing looks like? or is it metamorphic, or say, a doesn't have a 3rd dimensional form?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is delightfully constructed - it doles out the information steadily, switching format to avoid anything dragging (and without leaving major narrative gaps or repetition), and escalating the tension nicely. I can see the arguments KidF makes above, but they didn't affect my enjoyment overall. This is an object-lesson in good epistolary writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, after rereading it, I don't think it really adds much, and removing it doesn't create any sense of confusion. It's easy enough to pluck out, so I plucked it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair — I think I got the sense that the Foundation was willing to use the threat of termination largely because of how a lot of entries use threats of termination to control D-Class personnel, which implies to me that the Foundation is comfortable using the threat of violence to assure compliance. I imagined an organization like that would naturally feel comfortable using violence to assure compliance among researchers, too — particularly when the stakes are very high. I also imagined that the personnel operating the base aren't privy to what's actually going on (as it would be bad for morale), so the threat of termination served as a way of signaling to them that 'this is important, we're not telling you why, but don't fuck with it'.

That being said, it's not an incredibly important bit to me, and I don't think it necessarily adds much to the article. I don't like to heavily edit things I'm done with, but this is pretty tiny — I'll reread it in a few minutes and think about taking it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree; also thank you — I was particularly proud of just that paragraph. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! In retrospect, I think your criticisms are pretty spot-on — if I was rewriting this again, I think I'd dump the interview, dump the incident report, and focus almost exclusively on the Audio-Analysis bit — finding another way to (lightly) imply that this was some sort of attempt at a Russian super-weapon / destroy-the-world bluff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If threatening immediate termination isn't the Foundation's style, I'm fine changing it to something which fits them more closely!

It is for some people, not for others. It really comes down to headcanon. Maybe we need a #notmyfoundation tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundation personnel are expected to be professional and follow the containment procedures. That's the whole point. Adding an "or we'll kill you" is bad form.

It can be misleading to say that as professional. If the risk is high enough, then the threat of execution serves to show that level of risk.

Once, I would see termination as poor writing. But then again, the death penalty exists in many places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very new to this, so I'm totally willing to buy that I'm misrepresenting the Foundation as being absurdly authoritarian. That being said, the implication I was aiming for here was that the particular offenses which lead to immediate termination (broadcasting anything from the station other than the kill-signal) are offenses which could very foreseeably trigger another SCP-3034 incident — during which, children vanished world-wide, and the range of SCP-3034 doubled. Given that those are the stakes — that activations lead to some unknown entity kidnapping children (and possibly torturing them), while also increasing the range of SCP-3034 (which creates more opportunities for non-Foundation personnel to try and contact SCP-3034, leading to a heightened risk of additional incidents) — you don't think this standing order is reasonable?

Keep in mind, I don't think it's reasonable, but I'm a pacifist. That being said, I'm open to the notion I'm wrong — I'm new here, and I might be guilty of misreading the Foundation. The only point I wanted to get across with that bit was that the Foundation does not fuck around with this thing. It just fires the kill-signal whenever it comes up — nothing else. If threatening immediate termination isn't the Foundation's style, I'm fine changing it to something which fits them more closely!

EDIT: Oops, sorry, didn't see your edit till after I posted! I see what you mean — I was also imagining that this termination clause came after the incident, where a Foundation researcher acted somewhat irresponsibly (curiously trying to contact the entity, since no one had yet tried). This was their gut-reaction to that — "Wow, okay, NO tests, or we shoot you, we are 100% serious about this". That being said, if the problem is that Foundation personnel can be trusted to remain professional without resorting to threats of termination, then maybe I shouldn't have had a Foundation researcher decide to perform an unsanctioned test in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not nonsense. It's life and death. After what happens as a result of interaction, they cannot afford such a huge hit to normalcy. A doubling of the AOE, and another rash of missing children wouldn't bode well, and isn't something that would go unnoticed. That, plus whatever ungodly pit they're getting dragged to isn't somewhere anyone would be keen on sending people, let alone children. It's a threat. Nothing lol or over-the-top about it. Death for the offender seems justified given the situation.

Just because something could be used cheaply, or in 'outdated' articles doesn't make it something that can never be used ever again forever and ever no matter whatsies. It works here, and isn't even approaching poor-usage.
